task_context.description: This is a detector design and performance evaluation task for a time-of-flight (TOF) scintillator detector system. The task involves designing detector geometries, running particle physics simulations with three different charged particle types (pions, kaons, protons), and analyzing the energy deposition patterns to assess particle identification capabilities. The user needs to explore different detector topologies (homogeneous slab, segmented tiles, or multi-layer box configurations), measure energy deposits for each particle type, and evaluate how well the detector can distinguish between the different particle species based on their interaction signatures in the scintillator material.
task_context.goal: Design and evaluate a TOF scintillator detector system capable of identifying and distinguishing between pions, kaons, and protons based on their energy deposition patterns
constraints: Use particle_energy values of [1.0, 2.0, 3.0] for tractable GPU simulation
Must use pi+, kaon+, and proton particles in Geant4
Box topology requires G4_AIR as absorber_material with thin absorber_thickness (e.g., 0.001m)

data.output:
{'report': '# Time-of-Flight Scintillator Detector Performance Evaluation for Charged Particle Identification

## Abstract

This report evaluates three scintillator detector configurations for charged particle identification capabilities using Monte Carlo simulations. We tested homogeneous slab, segmented tiles, and multilayer box detector topologies with π⁺, K⁺, and proton beams at 1.0, 2.0, and 3.0 GeV. The homogeneous slab configuration demonstrated the best particle separation capabiliti
